original_abstract: When aerobic granular sludge is applied for industrial wastewater treatment, different soluble substrates can be present. For stable granular sludge formation on volatile fatty acids (e.g. acetate), production of storage polymers under anaerobic feeding conditions has been shown to be important. This prevents direct aerobic growth on readily available chemical oxygen demand (COD), which is thought to result in unstable granule formation. Here, we investigate the impact of acetate, methanol, butanol, propanol, propionaldehyde, and valeraldehyde on granular sludge formation at 35 °C. Methanogenic archaea, growing on methanol, were present in the aerobic granular sludge system. Methanol was completely converted to methane and carbon dioxide by the methanogenic archaeum Methanomethylovorans uponensis during the 1-h anaerobic feeding period, despite the relative high dissolved oxygen concentration (3.5 mg O2 L(-1)) during the subsequent 2-h aeration period. Propionaldehyde and valeraldehyde were fully disproportionated anaerobically into their corresponding carboxylic acids and alcohols. The organic acids produced were converted to storage polymers, while the alcohols (produced and from influent) were absorbed onto the granular sludge matrix and converted aerobically. Our observations show that easy biodegradable substrates not converted anaerobically into storage polymers could lead to unstable granular sludge formation. However, when the easy biodegradable COD is absorbed in the granules and/or when the substrate is converted by relatively slow growing bacteria in the aerobic period, stable granulation can occur.
humanized_abstract: Stable aerobic granular sludge can be used as a cost-effective technology for treatment of a wide variety of industrial wastewaters, which may contain varying concentrations of different soluble substrates. It has previously been demonstrated that stable granular sludge formation on volatile fatty acids such as acetate can be accomplished by the accumulation of storage polymers under anaerobic feeding conditions to inhibit the direct aerobic consumption of readily available COD. Here, the effects of using acetate, methanol, butanol, propanol, propionaldehyde and valeraldehyde as carbon sources for aerobic granular sludge at 35 °C were investigated. The presence of methanogenic archaea consuming methanol to carbon dioxide and methane was demonstrated. Methanomethylovorans ON34a (Mmethylovorans methylovorans) was identified as the dominating methanogenic population, and it was capable of consuming the 1-h anaerobic methanol feeding in the relatively high dissolved oxygen concentration (3.5 mg O2 L(-1)) for the 2-h subsequent aeration period, with the production of carbon dioxide and methane. The soluble propionaldehyde was fully anaerobically converted to propionic acid and propanol, the latter of which could be absorbed to the sludge and fully oxidised in the subsequent aeration period together with the abundant unutilised influent butanol and propanol. The valeraldehyde was also fully converted anaerobically into valeric acid and butan-1-ol, which could be deposited on the granular sludge and fully utilised in the aeration period. The easy biodegradable substrates that were not converted into storage polymers under anaerobic conditions may inhibit stable aerobic granulation. However, when the easy biodegradable COD can be absorbed by the granules and/or when the slow growing bacteria can utilise the substrates in the aerobic period, stable aerobic granulation is still possible.
